questions.questionText: The most common type of thyroid cancer is:
questions.optionB: Papillary carcinoma (60-80% — lymphatic spread, psammoma bodies, Orphan Annie nuclei, best prognosis)
questions.explanation: Papillary thyroid carcinoma: most common (60-80%). Features: papillary architecture, psammoma bodies (concentric calcifications), Orphan Annie eye nuclei (clear/ground-glass), nuclear grooves and inclusions. Spread: lymphatic (cervical LN metastasis). Excellent prognosis (>95% 10-year survival). Treatment: total thyroidectomy + radioactive iodine (RAI) ablation. Follicular: hematogenous spread (bone, lung). Medullary: C-cells, calcitonin, MEN2. Anaplastic: worst prognosis, elderly (rapidly fatal). Reference: Bailey & Love — Thyroid Cancer.

questions.questionText: The nerve most commonly at risk during thyroidectomy is:
questions.optionA: External branch of superior laryngeal nerve
questions.optionB: Recurrent laryngeal nerve (RLN — lies in tracheoesophageal groove, injury causes hoarseness/voice change)
questions.explanation: RLN: motor to all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except cricothyroid. Runs in tracheoesophageal groove. Closely related to inferior thyroid artery (key surgical landmark — ligate artery away from nerve). Unilateral injury: hoarseness (vocal cord paralysis). Bilateral injury: airway obstruction (stridor) — EMERGENCY tracheostomy. External branch of SLN (nerve of Galvani): injury → loss of high-pitched voice (cricothyroid paralysis). Identified by: ligating superior thyroid artery close to gland (not pole). Reference: Bailey & Love — RLN Injury.

questions.optionB: Inadvertent removal or devascularization of parathyroid glands → hypoparathyroidism
questions.explanation: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia: most common complication of total thyroidectomy. Causes: inadvertent parathyroidectomy (removal of parathyroid glands) or devascularization (disruption of blood supply). Usually transient (temporary hypoparathyroidism — days to weeks). Permanent: <2% in experienced hands. Symptoms: perioral tingling, numbness, carpopedal spasm, Chvostek sign (facial twitching), Trousseau sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff). Treatment: IV calcium gluconate (acute), oral calcium + calcitriol (maintenance). Reference: Bailey & Love — Post-thyroidectomy.

questions.questionText: Thyroid storm is a life-threatening condition treated with:
questions.optionB: PTU (propylthiouracil — blocks new hormone synthesis + peripheral T4→T3 conversion), propranolol, steroids, Lugol's iodine (given AFTER PTU), and supportive care
questions.explanation: Thyroid storm: extreme thyrotoxicosis (medical emergency, mortality 20-30%). Precipitants: surgery, infection, trauma, iodine contrast, non-compliance. Treatment sequence: (1) PTU (blocks synthesis + T4→T3 conversion, preferred over methimazole in storm), (2) β-blocker (propranolol — controls adrenergic symptoms + blocks T4→T3), (3) Lugol's iodine/SSKI (blocks hormone release — give 1 hour AFTER PTU to prevent new hormone synthesis), (4) Hydrocortisone (prevents adrenal crisis, blocks T4→T3), (5) Supportive care (cooling, fluids, ICU). Reference: Bailey & Love — Thyroid Storm.
